steps: Identify the catastrophe: What is the absolute worst thing you fear happening?
Assess probabilities: What is the actual likelihood of this event occurring?
Determine coping: If the worst happened, how would you manage/cope with it?
Evaluate the best-case and most likely scenarios to restore emotional balance.
Distinguish between what you can control right now and what is out of your hands.
Break down the massive task or situation into three tiny, bite-sized steps.
Identify the very first step (something that takes less than 5 minutes) and focus only on that.
Acknowledge the pain of feeling alone without judging yourself for it.
Practice 'Shared Humanity': Remind yourself that feeling lonely is a universal human experience, not a personal failure.
Identify one small, low-pressure act of kindness or micro-connection you can do for yourself today.
Take a conscious breath and note the physiological signals of anger.
Identify the 'should' rule that was violated (e.g., 'People should act exactly as I expect').
Reframe the situation from a broader, less personalized perspective to lower distress levels.
Identify any all-or-nothing cognitive distortions (e.g., 'Things will never get better').
Challenge the thought with objective evidence of times when things were okay.
Plan one simple activity that has historically brought a sense of accomplishment or pleasure.
Savor the positive feeling fully and acknowledge the effort that led to this state.
Write down the specifics of what is working well right now.
Anchor this feeling to draw on it during future moments of distress.
Name 5 things you can see around you.
Name 4 things you can physically feel.
Name 3 things you can hear.
Name 2 things you can smell.
Name 1 thing you can taste.
This refocuses your mind on the present moment and reduces racing thoughts.
